engineering.translation: For renunciates (yatīnām) freed from desire-and-anger, with subdued minds (yata-cetasām), who have realised the self — brahma-nirvāṇa is near (abhitaḥ vartate), surrounding them on all sides. The verse closes the brahma-nirvāṇa block (5.24-5.26) with abhitaḥ — 'on all sides, near, surrounding.' The state is not far; for the disciplined practitioner it is at hand. STRETCHED honestly because brahma-nirvāṇa exceeds the operational layer. The engineering analog scopes to: the senior who has cultivated the prior verses' practices — endured the kāma-krodha-udbhava (5.23), located satisfaction internally (5.21), recognised bounded enjoyments as bounded (5.22), seen with sama-darśana (5.18) — finds the settled state operationally near. The state is achievable iha eva (per 5.19) and abhitaḥ (per 5.26) — here, in this career, surrounding the senior who has done the work.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A senior engineer at twenty years tenure. The prior chapter-5 practices are operational: she has audited delight-sources (5.21-5.22), endured Slack-message urges in real-time (5.23), reviewed across the seniority gradient with uniform tone (5.18). Three months into a Datadog-tracked year that includes a CEO change Slack-announced, a product pivot, and a staffing reduction, she observes via her own GitHub-commit log that the settled state has held. The disturbances arrived; the disturbances did not produce the swings of younger years. The state — at the operational rung — is abhitaḥ, surrounding her, not far. She does not have to attain it through a special ritual; the practices have produced it as a continuous condition. STRETCHED honestly; the brahma-nirvāṇa metaphysics exceed. The operational rung is the readable signature: the state is at-hand for the engineer who has done the prior verses' work.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader hears 'near, on all sides' as 'easy.' The state is near for the practitioner who has done the prior chapters' work; for the engineer who has not, it is not near. The verse's claim is structurally conditional on the cultivation that produces it.
engineering.counter_example: When an engineer claims the abhitaḥ state without having done the cultivation — when the settledness is asserted rather than earned — the verse does not apply. The yatīnām (renunciates), yata-cetasām (mind-subdued), viditātmanām (self-realised) clauses are structurally significant; the abhitaḥ vartate follows from them, not in their absence.
engineering.implication: Audit whether the prior chapter-5 practices are cultivated. If yes, the state is at-hand. If no, the state is not near. The verse's claim is conditional.
engineering.quotable_line: For the engineer who has done the work, the settled state is abhitaḥ — at hand, on all sides. STRETCHED honestly.
